title: Recovering elliptic curves from their \(p\)-torsion
speaker: Benjamin Bakker
abstract: Given an elliptic curve \(E\) over a field \(k\), its \(p\)-torsion \(E[p]\) gives a 2-dimensional representation of the Galois group \(G_k\) over \(\mathbb F_p\). The Frey-Mazur conjecture asserts that for \(k= \mathbb Q\) and \(p > 13\), \(E\) is in fact determined up to isogeny by the representation \(E[p]\). In joint work with J.

proof_stages.label: Serre uniformity and Frey-Mazur motivation
proof_stages.summary: The talk motivates the main problem from Serre's open-image theorem, Serre's uniformity conjecture, and the Frey-Mazur expectation that large p-torsion Galois representations determine an elliptic curve up to isogeny.

proof_stages.label: Geometric function-field theorem
proof_stages.summary: The main theorem is stated: for curves of bounded gonality over an algebraically closed characteristic-zero field, non-isotrivial elliptic curves over their function fields are determined up to isogeny by their p-torsion representations for all sufficiently large p, with an analogous Shimura-curve/fake-elliptic-curve version.

proof_stages.label: Modular surface encoding p-torsion isomorphisms
proof_stages.summary: The surface Z_p=(X_p x X_p)/PSL_2(F_p) is introduced as the moduli space of pairs of elliptic curves with an isomorphism of p-torsion, Hecke curves inside it encode isogenies, and the theorem is reduced to proving that every bounded-gonality curve in Z_p factors through a Hecke curve.

proof_stages.label: Reduction to a ramification-versus-volume claim
proof_stages.summary: A bounded-gonality curve in Z_p gives a rational curve in a symmetric product, whose pullback to a cover by a product of modular curves yields a map alpha to P^1, and the central technical claim is that the ramification degree of alpha is o of the hyperbolic/canonical volume of the lifted curve.

proof_stages.label: Riemann-Hurwitz contradiction from small ramification
proof_stages.summary: Assuming the ramification claim, projection to a high-degree X_p factor gives a lower bound on 2g(C)-2 proportional to volume, while Riemann-Hurwitz for alpha bounds 2g(C)-2 above by ramification, giving a contradiction for large p unless the curve lies in the Hecke locus.

proof_stages.label: Ramification loci and two-part strategy
proof_stages.summary: The possible ramification is localized to coordinate diagonals and fixed-point loci of X_p x X_p to Z_p, namely CM points and bicusps, and the proof strategy is to combine hyperbolic repulsion of these special loci with volume costs for incidence along them.

proof_stages.label: Hyperbolic expansion and repulsion of CM points
proof_stages.summary: The speaker proves the model fact that the injectivity radius of X_p grows like 2 log p and sketches how closeness of same-type CM points forces bounded-height linear-algebra data, hence containment in bounded-degree Hecke divisors.

proof_stages.label: Incidence-volume estimates and final ramification bound
proof_stages.summary: Using Hwang-To style estimates, multiplicity at separated special points is controlled by volumes of embedded balls and clustered points are controlled via tubes around low-degree Hecke curves, so the CM contribution is o(volume), with cusps and diagonals treated similarly and the Shimura-curve case following from the CM analysis alone.

examples.name: Rational-field benchmark constants
examples.note: The cases k=Q, with expected Serre-uniformity bound 37 and Frey-Mazur bound 13, illustrate the conjectural uniform bounds without driving the later proof.
examples.engagement_reason: the rational-field constants 37 and 13 are cited with surrounding known theorems as benchmarks, but no computation or verification is carried out on them

passes.reason: The constants 37 and 13 are used as expected sharp threshold bounds over Q rather than as disproofs.

examples.name: Quaternionic fake elliptic curves
examples.note: Abelian surfaces with multiplication by an order in an indefinite quaternion algebra over Q are given as a concrete moduli problem parametrized by a Shimura curve.
examples.engagement_reason: fake elliptic curves from indefinite quaternionic multiplication are defined and given as a possible Shimura-curve moduli problem, without working through that example concretely

passes.reason: Quaternionic fake elliptic curves form a systematic Shimura-curve family distinguished by extra quaternionic endomorphism symmetry, not a sporadic exception or obstruction.
Quaternionic fake elliptic curves form a systematic Shimura-curve family of abelian surfaces with quaternionic multiplication, not a sporadic or extremal case.
Quaternionic fake elliptic curves form a systematic Shimura-curve family of abelian surfaces with quaternionic multiplication, not a sporadic or adversarial example.
Quaternionic fake elliptic curves are a systematic Shimura-curve family of abelian surfaces with extra quaternionic endomorphism symmetry, used as an extension case.
Quaternionic fake elliptic curves form a systematic Shimura-curve family with extra endomorphism symmetry, not a sporadic exception or obstruction.
Quaternionic fake elliptic curves form an infinite Shimura-curve family of abelian surfaces with extra endomorphism symmetry.
Quaternionic fake elliptic curves form a systematic Shimura-curve family of abelian surfaces with extra quaternionic endomorphism symmetry, used as another case of the theorem.
Quaternionic fake elliptic curves are an ordinary infinite-family Shimura-curve moduli example to which the theorem also applies.
They form an infinite Shimura-curve family of abelian surfaces with quaternionic multiplication, used to illustrate the theorem’s scope.
fake elliptic curves form a systematic Shimura-curve family of abelian surfaces distinguished by quaternionic multiplication, used as another case of the theorem
quaternionic fake elliptic curves form a systematic Shimura family with extra quaternionic endomorphism symmetry

examples.name: Multiple equatorial disk showing sharpness
examples.note: The speaker notes that m copies of an equatorial hyperbolic disk attain the point-incidence volume bound, illustrating its optimality rather than supplying a proof step.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker analyzes a curve given by m copies of an equatorial disk to show the volume–multiplicity bound is sharp

passes.reason: An m-fold equatorial disk is a nonreduced/totally geodesic symmetric object attaining the volume bound sharply, not refuting it.
An m-fold equatorial disk is a coincident multiple curve that attains the volume lower bound sharply rather than refuting it.
The m-fold equatorial disk is a highly regular multiple cycle that attains the volume bound sharply, but it is not used to refute the bound.
The repeated totally geodesic equatorial disk is the symmetric nonreduced equality case for the volume-multiplicity bound, showing sharpness but not refuting the bound.
The m-fold equatorial disk is a nonreduced totally geodesic disk attaining equality in the volume–multiplicity bound.
